name: The Tarot — Rider-Waite-Smith with Etteilla
description:
A complete 78-card tarot grammar holding two traditions in dialogue. The Rider-Waite-Smith deck (1909) provides the primary structure — 22 Major Arcana and 56 Minor Arcana with upright and reversed interpretations. Inlined alongside each card is its Etteilla correspondence (1788) — the first dedicated cartomancy system, which orders the Major Arcana by the seven days of Genesis rather than the Fool's Journey, and adds Etymology, Historical Context, Hermetic Correspondence, and Genesis Connection sections.

Where RWS asks 'what archetype is active in your life?', Etteilla asks 'what is the cosmos doing?' Both are valid maps of the same territory. This grammar lets you read either or both.

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: Pamela Colman Smith's original 1909 RWS illustrations (public domain as of 2021 in most jurisdictions), Etteilla's Grand Jeu deck illustrations (1788-1791, public domain), Marseille Tarot woodcuts (17th-18th century, public domain).

sections.Summary: Symbolizes innocence and new beginnings – a free spirit embarking on the unknown.
sections.Upright: Upright, The Fool represents innocence, new beginnings and free spirit. This card invites you to embrace these qualities, using them as a source of strength and motivation in your life.
sections.Reversed: Reversed, The Fool may signal recklessness, taken advantage of and inconsideration. This reversal invites reflection on underlying causes and beliefs contributing to such impulsive behavior. Consider how to make more considered choices going forward.
sections.Etteilla: In Etteilla's cartomancy system (1788), this position corresponds to 'La Folie (The Questioner - Female).' The fool's openness to experience. This card represents you as the questioner when seeking feminine wisdom. Approach your question with innocence and willingness to be surprised.

sections.Summary: Symbolizes willpower, desire, and the ability to manifest one’s intentions into reality.
sections.Upright: Upright, The Magician represents willpower, desire, creation and manifestation. This card invites you to tap into your personal power and focus your intentions, recognizing that you have the tools to create positive change in your life.
sections.Reversed: Reversed, The Magician may signal trickery, illusions and being out of touch. This reversal invites examining where you may be deceiving yourself or feeling powerless. Consider how to ground yourself in honesty and regain a sense of control.
sections.Etteilla: In Etteilla's cartomancy system (1788), this position corresponds to 'Le Magicien ou le Bateleur (The Magician/Juggler).' You have the tools and skills needed. The magician transforms will into reality through practiced craft. Like the street performer who masters sleight of hand, your abilities come from dedication and practice. Trust your abilities and take focused action.

metadata.etteilla_name: Le Magicien ou le Bateleur (The Magician/Juggler)

sections.Summary: Symbolizes intuition and the unconscious – the inner voice and hidden knowledge within.
sections.Upright: Upright, The High Priestess represents intuition, the unconscious and one’s inner voice. This card invites you to trust your gut feelings and pay attention to the subtle messages from your inner self, acknowledging the wisdom that comes from within.
sections.Reversed: Reversed, The High Priestess may signal a lack of center, lost inner voice or repressed feelings. This reversal invites exploring what distractions or anxieties are drowning out your intuition, and to create space for your true feelings to surface and be understood.
sections.Etteilla: In Etteilla's cartomancy system (1788), this position corresponds to 'Le Grand Prêtre (The High Priest).' The keeper of sacred knowledge and tradition. This card represents spiritual guidance, formal teachings, and the wisdom of established paths. Consider what traditions serve your growth and which teachers you trust.

sections.Summary: Symbolizes abundance, fertility, and nurturing – the creative, motherly energy of growth and prosperity.
sections.Upright: Upright, The Empress represents motherhood, fertility and nature. This card invites you to nurture yourself and others, fostering creativity and allowing abundance to grow in your life by caring for your physical and emotional needs.
sections.Reversed: Reversed, The Empress may signal dependence, smothering, emptiness or nosiness. This reversal invites considering where you might feel empty or overbearing – and to cultivate healthy independence and self-regard, rather than seeking validation solely from others.
sections.Etteilla: In Etteilla's cartomancy system (1788), this position corresponds to 'Les Plantes (Plants/Growth).' Life emerges and grows. This card speaks to natural development—things unfolding in their own time. Nurture what you've planted and trust the process of organic growth.
sections.Etymology: The Plants / Vegetation. From Latin 'planta' meaning plant, sprout, or cutting. Corresponds to Genesis 1:11-12, the creation of vegetation on the third day
sections.Historical Context:
Les Plantes embodies the third day of Genesis creation, when God commanded the earth to bring forth grass, herbs, and fruit-bearing trees. In Etteilla's system, this represents the first manifestation of organic life—the transition from abstract elements to living, growing things.

The Hermetic interpretation connects this card to the vegetable soul in Neoplatonic philosophy—the animating principle that distinguishes living matter from mere elements. Etteilla saw in vegetation the first evidence of divine life force manifesting in material form.
sections.Hermetic Correspondence: The vegetable soul; the life force animating organic matter

sections.Summary: Symbolizes authority, structure, and control – the fatherly influence of stability and responsibility.
sections.Upright: Upright, The Emperor represents authority, structure, control and fatherhood. This card invites you to develop clear limits and self-discipline, stepping into your own authority over your life while balancing firmness with compassion.
sections.Reversed: Reversed, The Emperor may signal tyranny, rigidity or coldness. This reversal invites examining issues with control or inflexibility in your life – possibly a tendency to be too harsh on yourself or others – and to work on introducing understanding and flexibility where it’s needed.
sections.Etteilla: In Etteilla's cartomancy system (1788), this position corresponds to 'Le Despote Africain (African Despot/King).' Power and its responsibilities. This card examines your relationship with authority—both your own and others'. Are you wielding power wisely, or being controlled by it?

sections.Summary: Symbolizes tradition, spirituality, and guidance – values, ethics, and the wisdom of collective teachings.
sections.Upright: Upright, The Hierophant represents tradition, conformity, morality and ethics. This card invites you to explore your belief systems and values – understanding the family or cultural traditions that shaped you – and to seek wise counsel or mentorship when needed.
sections.Reversed: Reversed, The Hierophant may signal rebellion, subversiveness or new approaches. This reversal invites reflection on question outdated beliefs and find your own authentic path. It suggests that breaking from convention can be healthy if your current norms no longer serve your growth.
sections.Etteilla: In Etteilla's cartomancy system (1788), this position corresponds to 'Le Grand Prêtre (The High Priest).' The keeper of sacred knowledge and tradition. This card represents spiritual guidance, formal teachings, and the wisdom of established paths. Consider what traditions serve your growth and which teachers you trust.

sections.Summary: Symbolizes love, union, and choice – the harmony and duality in partnerships and values.
sections.Upright: Upright, The Lovers represents partnerships, duality and union. This card invites you to explore how you form relationships and make heartfelt choices – aligning decisions with your values and fostering healthy bonds built on honest communication and mutual respect.
sections.Reversed: Reversed, The Lovers may signal a loss of balance, one-sidedness or disharmony. This reversal invites reflection on address imbalances in your relationships or inner conflicts in your values, working to restore honesty, self-love and harmony between the parts of your life that feel disconnected.
sections.Etteilla: In Etteilla's cartomancy system (1788), this position corresponds to 'Les Astres (The Celestial Bodies).' The celestial bodies have guided travelers for millennia. This card represents cosmic order and divine timing. Hope exists even in darkness. Follow what truly matters to you and trust the larger cycles of existence.
sections.Etymology: The Stars. From Latin 'stella' meaning star or celestial body. Corresponds to Genesis 1:14-19, creation of sun, moon, and stars
sections.Historical Context:
Les Étoiles depicts the fourth day of Genesis, when God created the sun, moon, and stars to govern day and night and to mark seasons, days, and years. For Etteilla, this was not merely astronomical but deeply astrological—the stars influence human destiny.

Etteilla's 'Société des Interprètes du Livre de Thot' systematically integrated astrology with tarot. The stars card embodies this synthesis: celestial bodies are not distant objects but active powers shaping earthly events.
sections.Hermetic Correspondence: The stellar intelligences; astrological influences on human affairs
sections.Genesis Connection: Day 4 - Creation of Sun, Moon, and Stars

sections.Summary: Symbolizes willpower, direction, and victory – harnessing inner drive to overcome obstacles.
sections.Upright: Upright, The Chariot represents direction, control and willpower. This card invites you to harness your determination and emotional balance to move forward. It suggests focusing on your goals and confidently steering through challenges by managing conflicting impulses within.
sections.Reversed: Reversed, The Chariot may signal a lack of control, lack of direction or aggression. This reversal invites examining where you feel pulled in opposing directions or stuck. It’s a reminder to regroup and clarify your goals, addressing inner conflicts or anxieties so you can regain a sense of direction.
sections.Etteilla: In Etteilla's cartomancy system (1788), this position corresponds to 'Le Ciel (The Heavens).' Step back and see the larger pattern. The heavens remind us that there's order in apparent chaos. Trust that things are unfolding as they should from a higher perspective.
sections.Etymology: The Heavens / The Sky. From Latin 'caelum' meaning sky, heavens, or celestial vault. Corresponds to Genesis 1:6-8, the creation of the firmament
sections.Historical Context:
Le Ciel represents the creation of the firmament—the great dome separating the waters above from the waters below. In ancient cosmology, this was understood as the physical structure of heaven itself, the boundary between earthly and divine realms.

In Hermetic philosophy, the celestial sphere contains the planetary intelligences that govern earthly affairs. Etteilla integrated astrological correspondences throughout his system, seeing the heavens as a living influence rather than mere backdrop.
sections.Hermetic Correspondence: The celestial sphere; the realm of planetary intelligences and fixed stars

sections.Summary: Symbolizes inner strength, compassion, and courage – gentle resilience taming the inner beast.
sections.Upright: Upright, Strength represents inner strength, bravery, compassion and focus. This card invites you to approach your challenges with patience and love. It highlights that true strength often lies in compassionate self-control – calming your inner critic or raw emotions with understanding rather than force.
sections.Reversed: Reversed, Strength may signal self-doubt, weakness or insecurity. This reversal invites reflection on acknowledge vulnerabilities without judgment. It suggests working on rebuilding confidence by treating yourself kindly, and remembering past instances of resilience to counter feelings of inadequacy.
sections.Etteilla: In Etteilla's cartomancy system (1788), this position corresponds to 'La Force (Strength).' True strength is quiet and patient. The ability to face challenges without aggression. Courage paired with compassion. Trust your inner power.
sections.Etymology: Strength / Fortitude. From Latin 'fortitudo' meaning courage, bravery, or strength of character. The third cardinal virtue; moral courage rather than mere physical power
sections.Historical Context:
La Force embodies the third Platonic virtue—fortitude or courage. This is not mere physical strength but moral courage: the capacity to face difficulty, danger, or pain without yielding to fear.

The traditional image often shows a figure with a lion, representing the mastery of animal instincts through gentle strength rather than violent domination.
sections.Hermetic Correspondence: Mastery of the animal nature; courage guided by wisdom

sections.Summary: Symbolizes introspection, wisdom, and guidance – the quest for truth through solitude and self-reflection.
sections.Upright: Upright, The Hermit represents contemplation, a search for truth and inner guidance. This card invites you to take time for yourself – to step back from the noise and reflect inward. It suggests that solitude and introspection can provide the insight or answers you seek at this stage of your journey.
sections.Reversed: Reversed, The Hermit may signal loneliness, isolation or feeling lost. This reversal invites reflection on reach out and reconnect where you’ve withdrawn too far. It’s a reminder that while introspection is valuable, support from others and sharing your experience is equally important when you feel adrift.
sections.Etteilla: In Etteilla's cartomancy system (1788), this position corresponds to 'Le Capucin (The Hermit/Monk).' Sometimes answers come only in solitude. The hermit's lantern illuminates inner truth. Take time for introspection. Seek wisdom within before seeking it outside.

sections.Summary: Symbolizes life cycles and change – the turning point of fate inviting adaptation and acceptance.
sections.Upright: Upright, the Wheel of Fortune represents change, cycles and inevitable fate. This card invites you to recognize what’s within your control and what isn’t. It invites acceptance of life’s ups and downs, reminding you that difficult times give way to better ones, and that adaptability and resilience are key as circumstances evolve.
sections.Reversed: Reversed, the Wheel of Fortune may signal feeling no control, clinging to control or bad luck. This reversal invites examining your resistance to change. It suggests working on letting go of the need to control every outcome, and reframing setbacks not as personal failures but as temporary phases that you have the strength to get through.
sections.Etteilla: In Etteilla's cartomancy system (1788), this position corresponds to 'La Roue de Fortune (Wheel of Fortune).' The wheel always turns. What goes up comes down, what goes down rises again. Understanding cycles helps you ride them rather than fight them. This is a turning point.

metadata.etteilla_name: La Roue de Fortune (Wheel of Fortune)

sections.Summary: Symbolizes fairness, truth, and accountability – the moral balance of cause and effect in one’s life.
sections.Upright: Upright, Justice represents cause and effect, clarity and truth. This card invites you to take accountability for your actions and to seek truth in your situation. It highlights the importance of honest self-reflection and making amends or adjustments to restore balance and fairness in your life where needed.
sections.Reversed: Reversed, Justice may signal dishonesty, unaccountability or unfairness. This reversal invites considering where you might feel treated unjustly or where you might be avoiding responsibility. It suggests working towards honesty with yourself and others, and understanding that healing often requires addressing imbalances or wrongs rather than ignoring them.
sections.Etteilla: In Etteilla's cartomancy system (1788), this position corresponds to 'La Justice (Justice).' Actions have consequences. This card asks for honest self-assessment. Are you treating yourself and others fairly? Balance the scales before moving forward.
sections.Etymology: Justice. From Latin 'justitia' meaning righteousness, equity, or lawfulness. One of the four cardinal virtues from Plato's Republic
sections.Historical Context:
Following the seven days of creation, Etteilla's trumps transition to the four cardinal virtues as described by Plato: Justice, Temperance, Fortitude, and Prudence. La Justice represents the first of these—the principle of righteous judgment and fair dealing.

In the Hermetic tradition, Justice connects to Ma'at—the Egyptian goddess of truth, balance, and cosmic order.
sections.Hermetic Correspondence: Ma'at; cosmic balance; the weighing of souls

sections.Summary: Symbolizes sacrifice, surrender, and a new perspective – a pause for insight by releasing control.
sections.Upright: Upright, The Hanged Man represents sacrifice, release and martyrdom (a willingness to let go). This card invites you to pause and allow a change of perspective. It suggests that by surrendering your usual approach or need for control, you can gain fresh insight into a problem – turning what feels like a setback into an opportunity for growth.
sections.Reversed: Reversed, The Hanged Man may signal stalling, needless sacrifice or fear of sacrifice. This reversal invites examining where you feel stuck or unable to let go of an old pattern. It’s a reminder that clinging too tightly out of fear can prevent progress – and that learning to release or see things differently, though uncomfortable, might be exactly what’s needed to move forward.
sections.Etteilla: In Etteilla's cartomancy system (1788), this position corresponds to 'La Prudence (Prudence).' Think before acting. This card counsels careful consideration and planning. Wisdom comes from learning from the past while preparing for the future.
sections.Etymology: Prudence / Wisdom. From Latin 'prudentia' meaning foresight, wisdom, or practical judgment. The fourth cardinal virtue; considered by Aristotle as 'practical wisdom'
sections.Historical Context:
La Prudence completes the quartet of cardinal virtues—representing practical wisdom, foresight, and good judgment. Aristotle called this 'phronesis,' the ability to discern the right course of action in particular circumstances.

The traditional image often shows a figure with a mirror (self-knowledge) and a serpent (wisdom).
sections.Hermetic Correspondence: Phronesis; practical wisdom; the inner counselor

sections.Summary: Symbolizes transformation, endings, and rebirth – the closing of one chapter to allow new growth.
sections.Upright: Upright, Death represents the end of a cycle, beginnings, change and metamorphosis. This card invites you to embrace necessary endings (such as the end of a habit, phase, or relationship) as a natural part of growth. It signifies that by processing the loss and letting go of what no longer serves you, you create space for new opportunities and personal transformation.
sections.Reversed: Reversed, Death may signal fear of change, holding on, stagnation or decay. This reversal invites exploring what fears are keeping you stuck in an unhealthy status quo. It suggests gently confronting your resistance to change, perhaps with support, and reframing endings not as something to dread but as transitions that can lead to renewal.
sections.Etteilla: In Etteilla's cartomancy system (1788), this position corresponds to 'La Mort (Death).' Transformation requires letting go. Something must end for something new to begin. This is not physical death but the necessary death of old patterns, beliefs, or situations.

sections.Summary: Symbolizes balance, moderation, and healing – finding the middle path through patience and integration.
sections.Upright: Upright, Temperance represents the middle path, patience and finding meaning through balance. This card invites you to practice moderation and to blend different aspects of your life or personality harmoniously. It suggests that healing often comes from integrating opposites (like work and rest, or emotion and reason) and taking a measured, patient approach rather than all-or-nothing thinking.
sections.Reversed: Reversed, Temperance may signal extremes, excess or lack of balance. This reversal invites reflection on identify areas where you may be overindulging or swinging between extremes. It’s a call to bring yourself back to center – perhaps by establishing routines, setting clear limits, or seeking a calmer middle ground – in order to restore equilibrium and peace of mind.
sections.Etteilla: In Etteilla's cartomancy system (1788), this position corresponds to 'La Tempérance (Temperance).' The art of mixing and moderation. Find the middle way between extremes. Patient blending of different elements creates something new. Healing through balance.
sections.Etymology: Temperance / Moderation. From Latin 'temperantia' meaning self-control, moderation, or sobriety. The second cardinal virtue; the golden mean between excess and deficiency
sections.Historical Context:
La Tempérance represents the second Platonic virtue—the principle of moderation and self-control that enables harmonious living. The traditional image shows a figure pouring liquid between vessels, representing the careful mixing and balancing of elements.

This alchemical symbolism was not lost on Etteilla, who saw in temperance the secret of transformation.
sections.Hermetic Correspondence: The alchemical mixing of elements; the art of proportion

sections.Summary: Symbolizes temptation, attachment, and the shadow self – the material or emotional chains that can bind one.
sections.Upright: Upright, The Devil acknowledges themes of addiction, materialism and playfulness (worldly indulgence). This card invites you to face and work through these shadow aspects with patience and patience. It asks you to recognize the habits or thought patterns that keep you feeling trapped – such as dependencies or a harsh inner voice – and to understand why they hold appeal, as a first step to regaining your power over them.
sections.Reversed: Reversed, The Devil can indicate freedom, release and restoring control. When this card appears reversed, it suggests an opportunity for relief and positive change. It points to a phase of breaking free – perhaps you are beginning to overcome an addiction, leave a toxic situation, or reclaim your autonomy. It urges you to continue letting go of old, limiting patterns and to embrace this newfound sense of empowerment and freedom.
sections.Etteilla: In Etteilla's cartomancy system (1788), this position corresponds to 'Le Diable (The Devil).' The chains that bind are often of our own making. This card reveals attachments, addictions, or patterns that keep you from freedom. Examine what you're bound to—material desires, unhealthy relationships, or limiting beliefs. Liberation comes through awareness.

sections.Summary: Symbolizes sudden upheaval and revelation – the collapse of false structures leading to profound change.
sections.Upright: Upright, The Tower represents sudden upheaval, a broken sense of pride and disaster (a rapid, unexpected change). This card invites you to understand that sometimes a dramatic shake-up can reveal truths that were hidden. It acknowledges the pain and shock of sudden change, yet also suggests that this breakdown can clear the way for more authentic growth once the dust settles. The focus is on finding stability within yourself when external stability crumbles.
sections.Reversed: Reversed, The Tower may signal a disaster avoided, delayed disaster or fear of suffering. This reversal invites exploring your fear of inevitable change – are you trying to prevent the unpreventable, or clinging to something that needs to fall apart? It suggests that while you might postpone the confrontation, true healing will come from facing the situation honestly. Recognize that the anticipation of change can be as distressing as change itself, and working with a counselor or support system can help you cope with that fear and build resilience.
sections.Etteilla: In Etteilla's cartomancy system (1788), this position corresponds to 'Le Temple Foudroyé (The Struck Temple).' Lightning strikes what cannot stand. Sudden disruption that, while shocking, clears away false structures. This is liberation through destruction of what was never solid.

sections.Summary: Symbolizes hope, faith, and renewal – a calm optimism and guiding light after the storm.
sections.Upright: Upright, The Star represents hope, faith and rejuvenation. This card invites you to nurture optimism and trust in the process of recovery. It’s the gentle reminder that after hardship (the Tower’s chaos), there is an opportunity for renewal – a chance to replenish your spirit, believe in yourself, and envision a positive future. This card invites you to engage in activities that restore you and to remain open to inspiration.
sections.Reversed: Reversed, The Star may signal faithlessness, discouragement or insecurity. This reversal invites reflection on acknowledge feelings of hopelessness or low self-esteem that may be weighing on you. It suggests working to reconnect with sources of inspiration and support. By identifying small glimmers of hope and practicing gratitude or tending to yourself, you can slowly rebuild your sense of faith in yourself and in better days ahead.
sections.Etteilla: In Etteilla's cartomancy system (1788), this position corresponds to 'La Lumière (Light/Enlightenment).' Truth and understanding emerge. What was hidden becomes visible. This is the moment of 'aha'—the insight that changes everything. Allow clarity to guide your next steps.
sections.Etymology: The Light / Enlightenment. From Latin 'lumen' meaning light, radiance, or understanding. Corresponds to Genesis 1:3 'Let there be light' - the first act of creation
sections.Historical Context:
La Lumière represents the first divine act in Genesis: 'And God said, Let there be light: and there was light.' In Etteilla's Hermetic interpretation, this is not merely physical illumination but the emergence of consciousness and understanding from the void.

The card draws from the Corpus Hermeticum, where light represents divine Nous (Mind) becoming aware of itself. Etteilla's system uniquely places this as card 2, immediately following Chaos, establishing a creation sequence that differs markedly from the Tarot de Marseille tradition.

In the 1843 'Jeu de la princesses' version, this card appears as 'Osiris or the Sun,' connecting to Egyptian solar theology.
sections.Hermetic Correspondence: The Divine Nous (Mind) of Hermetic philosophy; the first emanation of the Absolute

sections.Summary: Symbolizes intuition, illusion, and the subconscious – the realm of dreams, fears, and unseen depth.
sections.Upright: Upright, The Moon represents the unconscious, illusions and intuition. This card invites you to explore the deeper, less conscious parts of your psyche – such as your dreams, fears, and intuitions. It acknowledges that not everything is clear or rational, and that’s okay. This card invites you to sit with uncertainty, listen to your intuition, and perhaps use creative or reflective practices (like journaling, art, or therapy itself) to understand the undercurrents of your emotions without fear.
sections.Reversed: Reversed, The Moon may signal confusion, fear or misinterpretation. This reversal invites reflection on address the anxieties or misconceptions that may be clouding your perception. It suggests grounding techniques – like reality-testing your fears or talking them through with someone you trust – to dispel the shadows of doubt. By bringing these fears from darkness into light, you can reduce their power over you.
sections.Etteilla: In Etteilla's cartomancy system (1788), this position corresponds to 'Les Oiseaux et les Poissons (Birds and Fishes).' Movement through different realms with grace. Like birds in air and fish in water, find the element where you thrive. Adaptability and natural ease in your domain.
sections.Etymology: The Birds and the Fish. From Latin 'avis' (bird) and 'piscis' (fish). Corresponds to Genesis 1:20-23, creation of sea and sky creatures on day five
sections.Historical Context:
Les Oiseaux et les Poissons represents the fifth day of creation, when God filled the waters with fish and the sky with birds. These are the first animals—creatures that inhabit the fluid realms of water and air rather than solid earth.

In Etteilla's Hermetic symbolism, birds and fish represent mobility and freedom in their respective elements. Birds ascend toward the divine; fish explore the depths of the unconscious.
sections.Hermetic Correspondence: The elemental realms of Air and Water; mobility between states
sections.Genesis Connection: Day 5 - Creation of Birds and Fish

metadata.etteilla_name: Les Oiseaux et les Poissons (Birds and Fishes)

sections.Summary: Symbolizes joy, vitality, and optimism – the illumination of truth, success, and youthful energy.
sections.Upright: Upright, The Sun represents joy, success, celebration and positivity. This card invites you to embrace your authentic self and celebrate your achievements, no matter how small. It highlights the healing power of positivity and transparency – being open and childlike in your approach to life’s pleasures. The Sun invites you to step out of the shadows of doubt and into a place of warmth, confidence, and clarity about who you are.
sections.Reversed: Reversed, The Sun may signal negativity, depression or sadness. This reversal invites reflection on compassionately address any lingering sadness or pessimism. It suggests that even when you can’t feel the sunshine, it’s still there – meaning there are positive aspects in your life to rediscover. Counseling, creative expression, or simply reaching out to loved ones can help in dispelling the gloom. Work on challenging negative thoughts and letting your inner child have moments of play to gradually return to a sunnier outlook.
sections.Etteilla: In Etteilla's cartomancy system (1788), this position corresponds to 'Les Astres (The Celestial Bodies).' The celestial bodies have guided travelers for millennia. This card represents cosmic order and divine timing. Hope exists even in darkness. Follow what truly matters to you and trust the larger cycles of existence.
sections.Etymology: The Stars. From Latin 'stella' meaning star or celestial body. Corresponds to Genesis 1:14-19, creation of sun, moon, and stars
sections.Historical Context:
Les Étoiles depicts the fourth day of Genesis, when God created the sun, moon, and stars to govern day and night and to mark seasons, days, and years. For Etteilla, this was not merely astronomical but deeply astrological—the stars influence human destiny.

Etteilla's 'Société des Interprètes du Livre de Thot' systematically integrated astrology with tarot. The stars card embodies this synthesis: celestial bodies are not distant objects but active powers shaping earthly events.
sections.Hermetic Correspondence: The stellar intelligences; astrological influences on human affairs
sections.Genesis Connection: Day 4 - Creation of Sun, Moon, and Stars

sections.Summary: Symbolizes awakening, reckoning, and rebirth – a call to evaluate the past and renew one’s purpose.
sections.Upright: Upright, Judgement represents reflection, reckoning and awakening. This card invites you to engage in honest self-evaluation. It’s about reviewing where you’ve been and what you’ve learned, forgiving yourself and others, and using that understanding to make a positive fresh start. Judgement heralds a rebirth of sorts – an opportunity to take accountability for the past while not being defined by it, and to move forward with a clearer, more enlightened sense of purpose.
sections.Reversed: Reversed, Judgement may signal a lack of self-awareness, doubt or self-loathing. This reversal invites reflection on confront harsh self-judgments or a fear of change that may be holding you back. It suggests that you might be ignoring an inner calling or undervaluing your worth due to guilt or doubt. Working through these feelings – perhaps in therapy or through practices like patience exercises – can help you break free of stagnation. The goal is to learn the lessons from your past without punishing yourself. Consider how to answer life’s call to grow.
sections.Etteilla: In Etteilla's cartomancy system (1788), this position corresponds to 'Le Jugement Dernier (The Last Judgment).' The ultimate wake-up call. Time to honestly assess where you are and respond to a higher calling. The dead rise from their graves—what in you needs resurrection? This is not punishment but opportunity for renewal and rising.

sections.Summary: Symbolizes completion, wholeness, and fulfillment – the end of a journey and integration of lessons.
sections.Upright: Upright, The World represents fulfillment, harmony and completion. This card invites you to recognize how far you’ve come in your personal journey and to feel good about your achievements. It signifies a time of integration – where various parts of your life or psyche are coming together in unity. You are invited to savor this moment of wholeness and perhaps start dreaming of new horizons, knowing that you carry the wisdom of experience within you.
sections.Reversed: Reversed, The World may signal incompletion or lack of closure. This reversal invites considering what unfinished business or lingering regret might be preventing you from feeling complete. It suggests that you identify the loose ends – whether it’s seeking closure in a relationship, acknowledging an accomplishment without downplaying it, or letting yourself move on from an old goal that no longer serves you. By addressing these, you can find the satisfaction and closure needed to transition fully to your next phase.
sections.Etteilla: In Etteilla's cartomancy system (1788), this position corresponds to 'Repos (Rest).' After creation comes necessary rest. This is not laziness but essential integration. Allow what you've built to settle. Reflect before beginning anew.
sections.Etymology: Rest / Repose. From Latin 'repausare' meaning to rest, pause, or cease from labor. Corresponds to Genesis 2:2-3, the seventh day when God rested and made it holy
sections.Historical Context:
Le Repos represents the Sabbath—the seventh day when God rested from creation and sanctified rest itself. In Etteilla's system, this card serves as the female querent's significator, balancing Le Chaos which represents male questioners.

The 1865 Etteilla III booklet states: 'The true sense of this card is profound peace.' This is not mere inactivity but sacred completion—the rest that comes after work is finished.
sections.Hermetic Correspondence: The Sabbath; sacred rest; return to unity

sections.Summary: A card of creation, willpower, inspiration and desire that reflects the Fire element characteristics of the wands suit.
sections.Upright: Upright, Ace of Wands represents creation, willpower, inspiration and desire. This card invites you to seize the opportunity of a new passion or project with enthusiasm. It’s like the spark of motivation – you are invited to channel your energy into something meaningful, as this can boost your confidence and sense of purpose.
sections.Reversed: Reversed, Ace of Wands may signal a lack of energy, lack of passion or boredom. This reversal invites exploring why you feel blocked or uninspired. It suggests taking a step back to rest and rekindle your energy. You might need to experiment with small changes or creative exercises to reignite your motivation, focusing on rest instead of pushing forward forcefully when your heart isn’t in it.
sections.Etteilla: In Etteilla's cartomancy system (1788), this position corresponds to 'As de Bâton (Ace of Wands).' A powerful new beginning. Creative energy surges and a new opportunity presents itself. Seize this moment of inspiration and potential.

sections.Summary: A card of planning, making decisions and leaving home that reflects the Fire element characteristics of the wands suit.
sections.Upright: Upright, Two of Wands represents planning, making decisions and leaving home (venturing out). This card invites you to step out of your comfort zone thoughtfully. It’s about considering your options and formulating a plan for your future. You are invited to envision the life you want, weighing possibilities without fear, which can instill a sense of empowerment over your next steps.
sections.Reversed: Reversed, Two of Wands may signal fear of change, playing safe or bad planning. This reversal invites examining what fears are keeping you from making a move. It suggests that clinging to the familiar or second-guessing yourself might be limiting your growth. Working on confidence and flexible thinking – can help you overcome indecision and create a plan that feels secure yet progressive.
sections.Etteilla: In Etteilla's cartomancy system (1788), this position corresponds to 'Deux de Bâton (Two of Wands).' You hold the world in your hands. A decision point where you must choose your path. Consider your options carefully before moving forward.

sections.Summary: A card of looking ahead, expansion and rapid growth that reflects the Fire element characteristics of the wands suit.
sections.Upright: Upright, Three of Wands represents looking ahead, expansion and rapid growth. This card invites you to remain optimistic and forward-looking. It suggests you have set things in motion in your life; now it’s about maintaining momentum and being open to the opportunities that come. Psychologically, it’s an invitation to broaden your horizons – perhaps trying new experiences or trusting that your efforts will pay off – which can increase your confidence and resilience.
sections.Reversed: Reversed, Three of Wands may signal obstacles, delays or frustration. This reversal invites reflection on practice patience and adaptability. It acknowledges the disappointment of setbacks, encouraging you to reframe these as temporary hurdles rather than permanent failures. By managing expectations and maybe adjusting your plan, you can overcome the frustration and continue progressing, one step at a time.
sections.Etteilla: In Etteilla's cartomancy system (1788), this position corresponds to 'Trois de Bâton (Three of Wands).' Your plans are in motion. Watch the horizon as your ventures progress. This is a time of expansion and looking toward future possibilities.

sections.Summary: A card of community, home and celebration that reflects the Fire element characteristics of the wands suit.
sections.Upright: Upright, Four of Wands represents community, home and celebration. This card invites you to acknowledge and cherish the support systems and successes in your life. It’s about finding stability and joy in the present moment – perhaps celebrating a milestone, or simply enjoying time with family or friends. This card invites you to create or recognize a sense of belonging, which is deeply nourishing to your well-being.
sections.Reversed: Reversed, Four of Wands may signal a lack of support, transience or home conflicts. This reversal invites reflection on address feelings of instability or disconnect. It suggests having open conversations to resolve domestic or relationship tensions, or reaching out to broaden your support network. If you’re in transition (like moving or changing jobs) and feeling ungrounded, focus on small routines or rituals that help rebuild a sense of home within yourself, even as external circumstances shift.
sections.Etteilla: In Etteilla's cartomancy system (1788), this position corresponds to 'Quatre de Bâton (Four of Wands).' A time for celebration and gratitude. You've built something worth celebrating. Gather with community and honor this achievement.

sections.Summary: A card of competition, rivalry and conflict that reflects the Fire element characteristics of the wands suit.
sections.Upright: Upright, Five of Wands represents competition, rivalry and conflict. This card invites you to examine the role of conflict in your life. It may suggest that some tension or competition is normal as you assert yourself, but it’s important to handle disagreements constructively. This card invites you to develop healthy communication and conflict-resolution skills – seeing others not just as adversaries but as people with their own needs – which can turn competition into an opportunity in your life and teamwork.
sections.Reversed: Reversed, Five of Wands may signal avoiding conflict or respecting differences to a fault. This reversal invites considering if you are suppressing your own voice to keep peace, or if unresolved tensions are being swept under the rug. It suggests that constructive confrontation, though uncomfortable, might be necessary for real harmony. Practicing assertiveness in a safe environment (like therapy) can help you feel more confident about addressing conflicts openly, without excessive fear of rejection.
sections.Etteilla: In Etteilla's cartomancy system (1788), this position corresponds to 'Cinq de Bâton (Five of Wands).' Creative tension and competition. Different viewpoints clash, creating either productive debate or destructive conflict. Channel this energy constructively.

sections.Summary: A card of victory, success and public reward that reflects the Fire element characteristics of the wands suit.
sections.Upright: Upright, Six of Wands represents victory, success and public reward. This card invites you to acknowledge your accomplishments and allow yourself to feel proud. It signifies that receiving recognition – whether from others or simply giving credit to yourself – is healthy and reinforcing. You are invited to internalize positive feedback as evidence of your growth, which can boost self-esteem. This card reminds you that you *are* capable and that it’s okay to celebrate how far you’ve come.
sections.Reversed: Reversed, Six of Wands may signal excess pride, lack of recognition or a sense of punishment. This reversal invites exploring feelings of being overlooked or fears of failure. It suggests that you may need to derive a sense of worth from within rather than relying solely on external validation. If pride or ego is isolating you, humility and openness can restore balance. Conversely, if you’re feeling underappreciated, practice assertive communication about your contributions. The key is to maintain confidence without tipping into arrogance, and to seek acknowledgment in healthy ways.
sections.Etteilla: In Etteilla's cartomancy system (1788), this position corresponds to 'Six de Bâton (Six of Wands).' Success and public recognition. Your efforts are acknowledged and celebrated. Enjoy this moment of triumph.

sections.Summary: A card of perseverance, being defensive and maintaining control that reflects the Fire element characteristics of the wands suit.
sections.Upright: Upright, Seven of Wands represents perseverance, standing defensive and maintaining control. This card invites you to stand your ground on matters that are truly important to you, even if you feel challenged or outnumbered. It highlights resilience – that you have overcome challenges to get where you are, and you can continue to do so. However, it also invites you to check that your defensiveness is proportional: ensure you are protecting your boundaries and values in a healthy way without isolating yourself. This card is about asserting yourself with confidence born from knowing your worth.
sections.Reversed: Reversed, Seven of Wands may signal a urge to give up, destroyed confidence or feeling overwhelmed. This reversal invites reflection on address burnout and self-doubt. It suggests that constantly feeling on guard is exhausting – you might benefit from allowing support from others or choosing your battles more wisely. If your confidence has taken a hit, consider reflecting on past successes and seeking encouragement from allies. The goal is to regain your inner strength not by fighting continuously, but by restoring belief in yourself and your right to hold space in the world.
sections.Etteilla: In Etteilla's cartomancy system (1788), this position corresponds to 'Sept de Bâton (Seven of Wands).' You face opposition but have the advantage. Stand your ground and defend what you've worked for. Your position is stronger than it seems.

sections.Summary: A card of rapid action, movement and quick decisions that reflects the Fire element characteristics of the wands suit.
sections.Upright: Upright, Eight of Wands represents rapid action, movement and quick decisions. This card invites you to recognize periods of fast progress or change in your life. It’s about riding the wave of momentum: when things are moving forward, how can you stay grounded and focused? This card invites you to be adaptive and clear in communication as events unfold quickly. It’s an exciting energy, suggesting that you are capable of handling multiple things happening at once if you stay organized and true to your intent.
sections.Reversed: Reversed, Eight of Wands may signal panic, waiting or a slowdown. This reversal invites examining any anxiety about delays or the urge to rush that might backfire. It suggests practicing patience and breathing through the discomfort of waiting. If you’re feeling stuck, consider that this lull might be an opportunity to rest or gather information. If you’re panicking because things are not moving fast enough (or too fast), grounding techniques and mindful planning can help you regain a sense of control in a turbulent time.
sections.Etteilla: In Etteilla's cartomancy system (1788), this position corresponds to 'Huit de Bâton (Eight of Wands).' Things are moving quickly. Events accelerate, messages arrive, travel happens. Go with the flow of rapid progress.

sections.Summary: A card of resilience, grit and last stand that reflects the Fire element characteristics of the wands suit.
sections.Upright: Upright, Nine of Wands represents resilience, grit and making a last stand. This card invites you to acknowledge your perseverance through difficulties. It’s about that feeling of being tired but *still* finding the strength to protect what matters or to push through one more challenge. This card invites you to see yourself as a survivor of past trials – someone who can endure. At the same time, it’s a reminder to anticipate the need for rest and support; you don’t have to fight alone. Recognize how far you’ve come and that you have the inner resources to finish what you started.
sections.Reversed: Reversed, Nine of Wands may signal exhaustion, fatigue or questioning motivations. This reversal invites reflection on give yourself permission to rest and heal. It suggests that you may have been in “fight mode” for too long, leading to burnout or loss of drive. Reevaluate why you’re fighting – are those reasons still valid? It’s okay to adjust your goals or let some defenses down if they no longer serve you. Seeking help or delegating tasks isn’t defeat; it’s a strategic choice to recover your strength and clarity.
sections.Etteilla: In Etteilla's cartomancy system (1788), this position corresponds to 'Neuf de Bâton (Nine of Wands).' You've been tested and remain standing. Though weary, you have the strength to continue. Stay vigilant but don't become paranoid.

sections.Summary: A card of accomplishment, responsibility and burden that reflects the Fire element characteristics of the wands suit.
sections.Upright: Upright, Ten of Wands represents accomplishment, responsibility and burden. This card invites you to recognize when you are taking on too much. It acknowledges that you may have achieved a great deal (or are very dependable), but at the cost of feeling weighed down. This card invites you to prioritize and perhaps delegate some tasks. It’s okay to set down parts of your load. Celebrating what you’ve achieved is important, but so is ensuring that continuing success doesn’t come at the expense of your well-being.
sections.Reversed: Reversed, Ten of Wands may signal an inability to delegate, feeling overstressed or burnt out. This reversal invites reflection on take a serious look at your stress levels. It suggests that you might be carrying burdens that aren’t entirely yours to carry, or clinging to a belief that you must do it all alone. Challenge any guilt or perfectionism that prevents you from asking for help. Lightening your load – even a little – can restore your energy and allow you to rediscover the joy in your work or obligations, rather than being crushed by them.
sections.Etteilla: In Etteilla's cartomancy system (1788), this position corresponds to 'Dix de Bâton (Ten of Wands).' You've taken on much—perhaps too much. The weight of responsibilities may feel overwhelming. Consider what you can put down.

sections.Summary: A card of exploration, excitement and freedom that reflects the Fire element characteristics of the wands suit.
sections.Upright: Upright, Page of Wands represents exploration, excitement and freedom. This card invites you to approach life with curiosity and a willingness to learn. It’s the youthful spark in you that wants to try something new or set off on an adventure. Embracing this mindset can be healing – it helps you break out of ruts and find joy in discovery. This card invites you to give yourself permission to be playful and to follow your inspiration without overthinking.
sections.Reversed: Reversed, Page of Wands may signal a lack of direction, procrastination or creating conflict out of restlessness. This reversal invites reflection on what is holding you back from pursuing your interests. It could be fear of failure, or simply not knowing where to start, leading to frustration. It suggests setting small, attainable goals to regain a sense of direction, and being patient with yourself as you learn. Try channeling your restless energy into one focused project or hobby at a time, so you don’t inadvertently sabotage your progress by scattering your efforts.
sections.Etteilla: In Etteilla's cartomancy system (1788), this position corresponds to 'Valet de Bâton (Page of Wands).' Fresh enthusiasm and creative exploration. This card often brings news of new opportunities. Approach life with curiosity and openness.

sections.Summary: A card of action, adventure and fearlessness that reflects the Fire element characteristics of the wands suit.
sections.Upright: Upright, Knight of Wands represents action, adventure and fearlessness. This card invites you to be bold and pursue your passions with confidence. It signifies high energy and the courage to take risks. You are invited to harness this adventurous spirit to break through stagnation or fear. The Knight of Wands can inspire you to advocate for yourself or make that change you’ve been contemplating – trusting that you have the bravery to handle whatever comes.
sections.Reversed: Reversed, Knight of Wands may signal anger, impulsiveness or recklessness. This reversal invites reflection on pause and consider the consequences before acting. It suggests that underlying frustration or impatience might be driving you too hard. Developing approaches for anger (like taking a timeout, journaling, or speaking with a counselor) can prevent rash decisions. By balancing passion with awareness, you can still be courageous without endangering your goals or relationships.
sections.Etteilla: In Etteilla's cartomancy system (1788), this position corresponds to 'Chevalier de Bâton (Knight of Wands).' Passionate pursuit of goals with fearless energy. This card represents movement, travel, and taking action on your desires. Charge forward!

sections.Summary: A card of courage, determination and joy that reflects the Fire element characteristics of the wands suit.
sections.Upright: Upright, Queen of Wands represents courage, determination and joy. This card invites you to embody confidence and warmth. It suggests that you can be both strong-willed and compassionate – leading by example with your passionate energy. You are invited to trust in your ability to handle challenges while keeping a positive outlook. This queen’s presence in your life may indicate that nurturing your self-esteem and expressing your genuine enthusiasm will attract others’ respect and create an environment where you thrive.
sections.Reversed: Reversed, Queen of Wands may signal selfishness, jealousy or insecurities. This reversal invites reflection on look at any lingering self-doubt or need for external validation that could be manifesting as envy or defensiveness. It suggests rebuilding your confidence from within: focus on your own talents and values instead of comparing yourself to others. If you’ve been feeling withdrawn or uncharacteristically timid, work on reconnecting with activities and people that ignite your spirit. By healing your relationship with yourself, you’ll naturally release jealousy and step back into your authentic, vibrant self.
sections.Etteilla: In Etteilla's cartomancy system (1788), this position corresponds to 'Reine de Bâton (Queen of Wands).' Magnetic presence and creative confidence. This card represents someone who attracts success through warmth and self-assurance. Be bold in expressing yourself.

sections.Summary: A card of vision, leadership and overcoming challenges that reflects the Fire element characteristics of the wands suit.
sections.Upright: Upright, King of Wands represents a big-picture outlook, leadership and overcoming challenges. This card invites you to step into your power as a leader in your own life. It’s about having a clear vision for yourself and the determination to see it through. You are invited to trust in your experience and charisma – knowing that you can inspire others and navigate difficulties with maturity and courage. Psychologically, this King suggests balancing ambition with compassion, so that your drive uplifts both you and those around you.
sections.Reversed: Reversed, King of Wands may signal impulsiveness, being overbearing or unachievable expectations. This reversal invites reflection on moderate an excess of fiery energy. It suggests checking if you’re pushing yourself or others too hard, perhaps due to perfectionism or impatience. Try to reconnect with the original inspiration behind your goals and let go of the need to control every outcome. Cultivating empathy (listening to feedback, acknowledging others’ contributions) will prevent isolation. By setting realistic expectations and remaining adaptable, you can still achieve great things without burning out or alienating those who matter to you.
sections.Etteilla: In Etteilla's cartomancy system (1788), this position corresponds to 'Roi de Bâton (King of Wands).' A natural leader who inspires through passion and vision. This card represents mastery of will and creative enterprise. Take bold action with confidence.

sections.Summary: A card of new feelings, spirituality and intuition that reflects the Water element characteristics of the cups suit.
sections.Upright: Upright, Ace of Cups represents new feelings, spirituality and intuition. This card invites you to open your heart to new emotional experiences. It often signifies a time of healing or creative inspiration – an outpouring of positive emotion or love. You are invited to allow yourself to feel deeply and express those feelings, as doing so can be incredibly cathartic and nourishing. This card may also suggest exploring spiritual practices or creative outlets that let your emotions flow freely.
sections.Reversed: Reversed, Ace of Cups may signal emotional loss, blocked creativity or emptiness. This reversal invites reflection on address feelings of emptiness or a numbing of emotions. It suggests that you might be suppressing pain or avoiding vulnerability, which in turn blocks joy and creativity. By gently breaking down the walls around your heart – for instance, through patience or safe emotional expression – you create space for new positive feelings to enter again.
sections.Etteilla: In Etteilla's cartomancy system (1788), this position corresponds to 'As de Coupe (Ace of Cups).' The heart opens to new love and emotional experience. Overflowing feelings, spiritual connection, and the beginning of deep bonds.

sections.Summary: A card of unity, partnership and connection that reflects the Water element characteristics of the cups suit.
sections.Upright: Upright, Two of Cups represents unity, partnership and connection. This card invites you to nurture balanced, loving relationships – whether romantic, friendship, or even the relationship you have with yourself. It’s about mutual respect and emotional exchange. You are invited to practice open communication and trust, as doing so can deepen bonds and promote healing for both parties. This card also reminds you to see the value in collaboration and support; you don’t have to do everything alone when there are people who care about you.
sections.Reversed: Reversed, Two of Cups may signal imbalance, broken communication or tension in a relationship. This reversal invites reflection on address miscommunications or unequal effort in your partnerships. It suggests having honest conversations about needs and feelings, possibly with the guidance of a counselor if conflicts are deep. Also, if a relationship has ended or trust was broken, this card urges tending to yourself and possibly seeking forgiveness – not necessarily to rekindle the relationship, but to release resentment and restore your own peace. Healing relational wounds takes time, but acknowledging them is the first step.
sections.Etteilla: In Etteilla's cartomancy system (1788), this position corresponds to 'Deux de Coupe (Two of Cups).' A beautiful connection between two people. Whether romantic or platonic, this represents mutual respect, attraction, and partnership.

sections.Summary: A card of friendship, community and happiness that reflects the Water element characteristics of the cups suit.
sections.Upright: Upright, Three of Cups represents friendship, community and happiness. This card invites you to lean into social support and celebrate connections. It highlights the healing power of friendship and sharing – whether it’s good news or burdens – with those who uplift you. You are invited to take time to enjoy the company of people who accept you as you are. This card also suggests that participating in group activities or community events can bring joy and alleviate feelings of isolation, reminding you that you’re part of a larger whole.
sections.Reversed: Reversed, Three of Cups may signal overindulgence, gossip or isolation. This reversal invites reflection on look at how you’re relating to your social circle. It’s possible you might be withdrawing out of mistrust or, on the other hand, engaging in superficial social interactions that leave you unfulfilled. If isolation is the issue, consider reaching out to a trusted friend or counselor to break the ice of loneliness. If drama or overindulgence is the issue (like partying as a managing mechanism or getting caught in gossip), think about what feelings you might be avoiding. Strive for authentic connections and balanced social life that genuinely nourish you.
sections.Etteilla: In Etteilla's cartomancy system (1788), this position corresponds to 'Trois de Coupe (Three of Cups).' Joyful gathering with friends and community. Celebrate together, collaborate, and share in each other's happiness. Friendship enriches life.

sections.Summary: A card of apathy, contemplation and disconnectedness that reflects the Water element characteristics of the cups suit.
sections.Upright: Upright, Four of Cups represents apathy, contemplation and feeling disconnected. This card invites you to explore why you might be feeling indifferent or emotionally flat. It often appears when we are so deep in introspection or dissatisfaction that we don’t see positive opportunities around us. This card invites you to practice gratitude for small things and to gently shake up your routine – even a minor change or trying a new activity can break the pattern of stagnation. It’s a reminder that while it’s okay to turn inward for a while, staying in that state too long may cause you to miss out on sources of fulfillment being offered to you.
sections.Reversed: Reversed, Four of Cups may signal a sudden awareness, choosing happiness or acceptance. This reversal indicates that you might be ready to re-engage with life after a period of withdrawal. It invites you to take the lessons from your contemplation and start saying “yes” to things again – reaching out to friends, pursuing a hobby, or simply noticing what’s good in your day-to-day. Embrace this renewed openness. If you’ve made peace with a past disappointment, allow that acceptance to lighten your mood and guide you back into connection with the world around you.
sections.Etteilla: In Etteilla's cartomancy system (1788), this position corresponds to 'Quatre de Coupe (Four of Cups).' An offer is being made that you may not see. Step back from apathy and look around. What opportunities are you ignoring?

sections.Summary: A card of loss, grief and self-pity, highlighting the need to confront these emotions on the path to healing.
sections.Upright: Upright, Five of Cups acknowledges themes of loss, grief and self-pity. This card invites you to face and work through these painful emotions with patience and patience, rather than avoiding them. It’s normal to mourn what’s been lost. At the same time, this card gently reminds you that not *all* is lost – in many depictions, some cups remain standing. In practice, this means that when you’re ready, you can begin to notice the support or opportunities still present in your life. Allowing yourself to grieve fully is an important step toward eventually finding acceptance and hope again.
sections.Reversed: Reversed, Five of Cups may signal acceptance, moving on or finding peace. This reversal indicates that you may be turning a corner in your process of recovery. It invites you to acknowledge the progress you’ve made in overcoming sadness – perhaps you’ve forgiven someone, or yourself, or you’ve decided to embrace the present rather than living in regret. You might celebrate small moments of relief or the return of interest in life’s pleasures. As you continue to let go of past disappointments, you create more room for optimism and emotional freedom.
sections.Etteilla: In Etteilla's cartomancy system (1788), this position corresponds to 'Cinq de Coupe (Five of Cups).' Loss and disappointment are real, but not everything is lost. Grieve what's gone, but notice what remains. The bridge home is still there.

sections.Summary: A card of familiarity, happy memories and healing that reflects the Water element characteristics of the cups suit.
sections.Upright: Upright, Six of Cups represents familiarity, happy memories and healing. This card invites you to reflect on positive aspects of your past – perhaps childhood joys or simpler times – as a source of comfort and insight. It’s about reconnecting with your inner child and remembering the activities or values that made you feel secure and happy. This card invites you to bring some of those gentle, nostalgic elements into your present life (like revisiting an old hobby or reaching out to an old friend) as a way to heal and remind yourself of how far you’ve come.
sections.Reversed: Reversed, Six of Cups may signal moving forward, leaving home or developing independence. This reversal indicates a readiness to let go of the past in order to grow. It invites you to gently cut the cords of nostalgia that might be holding you back – for instance, outdated self-images or old comfort zones. While honoring your past, you’re encouraged to focus on the present and future. If you’ve been clinging to how things “used to be,” this is a sign that you can create new positive experiences. Embrace your independence and trust that you can build supportive relationships and happy memories in the here and now, too.
sections.Etteilla: In Etteilla's cartomancy system (1788), this position corresponds to 'Six de Coupe (Six of Cups).' Sweet memories and connections to the past. Reunions with old friends or returning to familiar places. Honor your roots while living in the present.

sections.Summary: A card of searching for purpose, choices and daydreaming that reflects the Water element characteristics of the cups suit.
sections.Upright: Upright, Seven of Cups represents searching for purpose, choices and daydreaming. This card invites you to explore your desires and fantasies, but also to stay grounded when making decisions. It’s great to envision different possibilities for your life – this can be a creative and hopeful exercise – but the card invites you to eventually pick a direction and commit to it. Psychologically, it warns against the paralysis that can come from too many options or from idealizing outcomes. You’re invited to clarify what truly matters to you (perhaps with a pros/cons list or talking it out) so that your pursuit of purpose becomes manageable and real, rather than just a daydream.
sections.Reversed: Reversed, Seven of Cups may signal a lack of purpose, diversion or confusion. This reversal invites reflection on confront feelings of aimlessness or overwhelm. It suggests that you may have been avoiding making a choice or confronting reality by escaping into distractions or fantasies. This card gently urges you to come back to center. Setting small, concrete goals can help regain a sense of direction. By eliminating some distractions and focusing on one or two priorities, you can clear the fog of confusion. The process might involve letting go of appealing-but-unrealistic options and committing to a path that, while not perfect, will bring you more fulfillment than staying in indecision.
sections.Etteilla: In Etteilla's cartomancy system (1788), this position corresponds to 'Sept de Coupe (Seven of Cups).' Many options present themselves, but not all are real. Distinguish between fantasy and achievable goals. Beware of wishful thinking.

sections.Summary: A card of walking away, disillusionment and leaving behind that reflects the Water element characteristics of the cups suit.
sections.Upright: Upright, Eight of Cups represents walking away, disillusionment and leaving behind. This card invites you to recognize when something in your life is no longer fulfilling or healthy, and to allow yourself to move on from it. This could mean leaving a bad situation, letting go of a long-held but limiting belief, or withdrawing your energy from unreciprocated relationships. It’s a brave act of honoring what you need. You are invited to trust that this journey away – though it may be sad or lonely – is toward a more authentic and satisfying life. It acknowledges the courage it takes to step into the unknown in search of greater emotional honesty or fulfillment.
sections.Reversed: Reversed, Eight of Cups may signal avoidance, fear of change or fear of loss. This reversal invites exploring what’s keeping you stuck in an unhappy place. It suggests a fear of the unknown or guilt about leaving might be trapping you in a situation that’s draining you. Working through those fears (perhaps by envisioning the worst-case versus reality, or building a support plan for change) can empower you. Sometimes we need to affirm that it’s okay to put ourselves first. This card reversal is a nudge that you might already know you need to move on, and with compassionate support, you can find the strength to do so and prevent greater loss to your well-being.
sections.Etteilla: In Etteilla's cartomancy system (1788), this position corresponds to 'Huit de Coupe (Eight of Cups).' Something that once fulfilled you no longer does. It's time to walk away in search of deeper meaning. Leave the comfortable for the essential.

sections.Summary: A card of satisfaction, emotional stability and luxury that reflects the Water element characteristics of the cups suit.
sections.Upright: Upright, Nine of Cups represents satisfaction, emotional stability and luxury. This card invites you to acknowledge and enjoy the contentment you have created in your life. It’s often called the “wish card,” implying that you should allow yourself to savor accomplishments and the good things around you. This isn’t about complacency, but about practicing gratitude and recognizing that it’s okay to feel proud and happy. You are invited to embrace moments of pleasure without guilt – such as treating yourself or celebrating personal milestones – which reinforces a positive outlook and self-worth.
sections.Reversed: Reversed, Nine of Cups may signal a lack of inner joy, smugness or dissatisfaction. This reversal invites reflection on look beneath the surface of your wishes. It’s possible you achieved what you thought you wanted, yet still feel unfulfilled – which suggests those goals might not have aligned with your deeper needs. Or perhaps contentment has turned into stagnation, or pride into taking things for granted. It suggests refocusing on authentic happiness: identify what truly brings you joy versus what merely impresses others. By realigning your life with your core values and practicing humility and openness, you can convert hollow victories into genuine fulfillment.
sections.Etteilla: In Etteilla's cartomancy system (1788), this position corresponds to 'Neuf de Coupe (Nine of Cups).' The wish card. Your desires are being fulfilled. Take time to appreciate and enjoy your blessings. Satisfaction is yours.

sections.Summary: A card of inner happiness, fulfillment and dreams coming true that reflects the Water element characteristics of the cups suit.
sections.Upright: Upright, Ten of Cups represents inner happiness, fulfillment and dreams coming true. This card invites you to cherish the sense of emotional security and love in your life. It often points to family or community harmony – the feeling of being truly at home with yourself and others. You are invited to acknowledge moments of contentment and to let yourself believe in lasting happiness (not in a naive way, but as something that can be real and deserved). This card suggests that by nurturing your relationships and staying true to your heart, you contribute to a lasting sense of peace and joy for everyone involved.
sections.Reversed: Reversed, Ten of Cups may signal shattered dreams, broken family or domestic disharmony. This reversal invites reflection on address conflicts or unmet expectations within your closest circles. It suggests that the image of perfect happiness might have cracked – and that’s painful, but it can be healed or redefined. Family therapy or honest conversations could be beneficial if there’s strife in the home. If a long-held dream has been lost, grief work is important here as well. Ultimately, this card asks you not to give up on the idea of happiness; rather, to work through the current difficulties and remember that a new form of contentment may emerge on the other side of conflict or disappointment.
sections.Etteilla: In Etteilla's cartomancy system (1788), this position corresponds to 'Dix de Coupe (Ten of Cups).' The rainbow after the storm. Emotional fulfillment, happy relationships, and contentment. This is the heart's true desire realized.

sections.Summary: A card of happy surprise, dreamer and sensitivity that reflects the Water element characteristics of the cups suit.
sections.Upright: Upright, Page of Cups represents a happy surprise, being a dreamer and sensitivity. This card invites you to stay open-hearted and receptive to emotional experiences. It often implies a message or creative inspiration is coming your way, so allow your intuition to speak. You are invited to nurture your inner child – that part of you that is imaginative, gentle, and unafraid to feel. This might mean trying a creative hobby, writing down your dreams, or simply approaching situations with curiosity rather than judgment. Embracing your sensitive side can bring delightful insights and new emotional richness into your life.
sections.Reversed: Reversed, Page of Cups may signal emotional immaturity, insecurity or disappointment. This reversal invites exploring areas where you might feel overly vulnerable or where emotional growth has been stunted. It suggests that unresolved insecurities could be causing you to either withdraw or overreact. Consider developing healthier ways to express your feelings – possibly with the help of someone you trust or through journaling – so that disappointments don’t fester into bitterness. Learning emotional steadying practices and building self-esteem will help turn those insecurities into opportunities in your life, allowing you to reconnect with others from a place of confidence and authenticity.
sections.Etteilla: In Etteilla's cartomancy system (1788), this position corresponds to 'Valet de Coupe (Page of Cups).' Emotional or intuitive messages arrive. Stay open to creative inspiration and honor your sensitive nature. Something unexpected emerges.

sections.Summary: A card of following the heart, idealism and romance that reflects the Water element characteristics of the cups suit.
sections.Upright: Upright, Knight of Cups represents following the heart, idealism and being romantic. This card invites you to stay connected with your emotional intelligence and empathy as you pursue your goals. It’s about valuing intuition and compassion in your actions. You are invited to allow yourself to feel deeply and perhaps to express affection or creativity openly. This knight suggests that your journey forward can be guided by your ideals and dreams – just be sure to balance them with a bit of practicality so you don’t get lost in fantasy. Overall, it’s a reminder that acting with kindness and love is a strength, not a weakness.
sections.Reversed: Reversed, Knight of Cups may signal moodiness or disappointment. This reversal invites reflection on check in with your emotional balance. It suggests you may be experiencing intense highs and lows or feeling let down by someone (or by your own high expectations). Working on emotional regulation techniques – such as awareness, or simply naming and accepting your feelings without judgment – can help smooth out the mood swings. Additionally, if disappointment in love or creativity is an issue, consider where unrealistic expectations might be adjusted. The goal is to find a steadier flow for your feelings, so that temporary blues or frustrations don’t derail your overall journey toward emotional fulfillment.
sections.Etteilla: In Etteilla's cartomancy system (1788), this position corresponds to 'Chevalier de Coupe (Knight of Cups).' An offer or invitation arrives, often romantic. Follow your heart and pursue your dreams. Someone approaches with genuine feeling.

sections.Summary: A card of compassion, calm and comfort that reflects the Water element characteristics of the cups suit.
sections.Upright: Upright, Queen of Cups represents compassion, calm and comfort. This card invites you to cultivate a nurturing and empathetic approach towards yourself and others. It’s about emotional maturity – being deeply in tune with feelings without being overwhelmed by them. You are invited to trust your intuition and to offer or seek support in a gentle way. This queen suggests that creating a safe emotional space (perhaps through therapy, meditation, or time in nature) will allow your creativity and intuition to flourish. Embrace your role as someone who can heal and be healed through caring connection.
sections.Reversed: Reversed, Queen of Cups may signal martyrdom, insecurity or dependence. This reversal invites examining boundaries in your emotional life. You might be giving too much of yourself and feeling drained, or relying too heavily on others for validation. It suggests working on tending to yourself and asserting your needs: you can be compassionate without self-sacrificing to an unhealthy degree. If insecurity is an issue, exploring its roots with someone you trust might help – perhaps past wounds are causing you to doubt your worth or cling to others. The aim is to restore your inner balance so that your empathy remains a gift and not a source of personal depletion or confusion.
sections.Etteilla: In Etteilla's cartomancy system (1788), this position corresponds to 'Reine de Coupe (Queen of Cups).' Deep intuition and emotional wisdom. Trust your inner knowing and nurture yourself and others. Your sensitivity is your strength.

sections.Summary: A card of compassion, control and balance that reflects the Water element characteristics of the cups suit.
sections.Upright: Upright, King of Cups represents compassion, control and balance (especially emotional balance). This card invites you to develop emotional mastery – to feel your feelings fully, but respond to them wisely rather than react impulsively. You are invited to be both empathetic and stable, the way a good mentor or parent figure would be. This might manifest as taking a few deep breaths before responding in a conflict, or being the “calm center” in a storm for yourself and loved ones. It’s a reminder that true strength lies in understanding emotions without letting them rule you, and using that understanding to guide loving actions.
sections.Reversed: Reversed, King of Cups may signal coldness, moodiness or bad advice. This reversal invites reflection on address any emotional disconnection or volatility. It suggests you might be bottling things up and then experiencing sudden mood swings, or perhaps emotionally manipulating situations without realizing it. The card asks you to be honest about feelings you might be denying. You could benefit from expressing vulnerability in a safe space, instead of maintaining a frosty exterior. Also, be cautious of advice you give or receive while in an emotionally unstable state – it’s okay to seek a second opinion or delay important decisions until you feel more centered. By working on your emotional literacy (naming and understanding your emotions), you can return to a place of compassion and equilibrium.
sections.Etteilla: In Etteilla's cartomancy system (1788), this position corresponds to 'Roi de Coupe (King of Cups).' Emotional intelligence and mature wisdom. This card represents someone who navigates feelings with grace. Balance heart and head in your decisions.

sections.Summary: A card of breakthrough, clarity and sharp mind that reflects the Air element characteristics of the swords suit.
sections.Upright: Upright, Ace of Swords represents breakthrough, clarity and a sharp mind. This card invites you to embrace clear thinking and honest communication. It often signals an “aha” moment – a new insight or realization that can cut through confusion. You are invited to use this clarity to speak your truth or make a decision you’ve been weighing. This card highlights the power of the mind to understand and to articulate what’s right for you. It’s an opportunity to set limits or plans with precision, backed by a sense of intellectual empowerment and truth.
sections.Reversed: Reversed, Ace of Swords may signal confusion, brutality or chaos. This reversal invites reflection on address mental fog or destructive thoughts.
